name: PyInstaller (Bundle Python Apps into Executables)
pricing_notes: Open source (GPL-2.0 with commercial exception); PyInstaller team

performance.notes: Build tool; execution speed of resulting binary depends on app; startup adds ~1-2s for unpacking

agent_readiness.notes: Freeze Python apps into standalone executables. pyinstaller script.py. pyinstaller --onefile script.py. pyinstaller --windowed gui.py (no console). pyinstaller --add-data 'assets:assets' script.py. .spec file: spec gives full control. pyinstaller script.spec. UPX compression: pyinstaller --upx-dir=/path/to/upx. Hidden imports: --hidden-import=pkg. Cross-platform: run on target OS. Result: dist/script/ (folder) or dist/script (single-file). Single-file extracts to temp on run. Antiviruses often flag single-file binaries.

gotchas: Must build on target OS — no cross-compilation (build Windows .exe on Windows)
Antivirus software commonly flags single-file PyInstaller binaries as false positives
Dynamic imports and plugin systems may require --hidden-import
Single-file mode extracts to temp directory on launch (slow startup)
GPL-2.0 license — bootloader is GPL but commercial exception allows proprietary apps
best_when: Distributing Python scripts as standalone executables to non-technical users
CLI tools that need to run without Python installed
Packaging desktop GUI apps (Tkinter, Qt) for distribution
Internal tooling that needs no-install deployment
avoid_when: When Nuitka's compiled performance is needed
Cross-platform builds from single machine (requires per-OS builds)
If antivirus false positives are a concern (use code signing)
summary: PyInstaller bundles Python apps into standalone executables. pyinstaller --onefile script.py creates single binary; --windowed for GUI without console. .spec file for full control. --add-data for assets, --hidden-import for dynamic imports. Must build on target OS. GPL-2.0 with commercial exception. Antivirus false positives common with --onefile. Most popular Python-to-executable tool for distributing to non-Python users.
